# CrossMath Puzzle Generator ‚Äî Project Specification

## Overview

### Goal

Build a Java-based algorithm that automatically generates valid Crossmath / Crossmatch-style puzzles on a 2D sparse grid.

The generator should first create a fully solved puzzle, then support a later step where some values/operators/results are removed to create a playable puzzle.

The main purpose is to eliminate manual puzzle creation and replace it with a configurable, efficient generator that can produce many valid games under number and operator constraints.

The puzzle will be able to create valid standart games for adults (four operators, numbers up to 100) as well as early grade education (limited number of operators and number range). Like first grade students can only play addition and numbers up to ten. But eventually even more complex operators will be introduced - like modulo exp log and number range up to 1000. The games will support all of this customization. We will add grading later.

The generator shall produce random but valid solved puzzles.

Randomness should be controllable by:

seed
reproducible generation mode

This is important for debugging and test repeatability.

## Puzzle Model
### Core structure

The puzzle is a sparse 2D grid containing equations of the form:

A op B = C

Equations may be placed horizontally or vertically.

Important rule:

Equations do not rely on operator precedence.
Each equation is isolated in meaning.
The only intersections happen where cells are shared physically in the grid.
A shared cell must have the same value/operator token for all equations that use it.

This is not a dense arithmetic-expression matrix. It is a set of valid equations embedded in a grid.

### Supported early scope

Initial version should support:

addition
subtraction
multiplication
division

Later extensions:

exponent
square root
min
max
avg
custom unary or binary operators
3-digit numbers
### Grid flexibility

The generator must not assume one rigid fixed shape.

It should support:

classic small grids
3x3-style or 9-cell-style arrangements if desired
sparse layouts with holes
optional removal of full equations from the shape
variable equation placements as long as they fit inside the grid and remain valid

This flexibility is part of the puzzle variety strategy.


---

## Package

All classes live in `io.crossmath.engine`.

---

## Architecture

### Core pipeline

```
PuzzleConfig  ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí  CrossMathGenerator  ‚Üí  PuzzleGrid
OperatorRegistry  ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí                              ‚Üì
BracketedPicker   ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí                        PuzzlePrinter
                                                         EquationMask
```

### File inventory

| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `PuzzleConfig` | All derived constants from root inputs; per-operator caps; grading fields; built via Builder |
| `Operator` (interface) | Strategy: `apply()` + `validRightOperands()` |
| `AddOperator` | + |
| `SubtractOperator` | ‚àí |
| `MultiplyOperator` | √ó with BitSet prime sieve |
| `DivideOperator` | √∑ exact integer only |
| `CustomOperators` | Min, Max, Exp, Sqrt stubs (ready to activate) |
| `OperatorRegistry` | `LinkedHashMap<Character,Operator>` ‚Äî O(1) lookup, unique symbols; filters by `allowedOperators` |
| `BracketedPicker` | Round-robin bracket selection for value variety |
| `PuzzleGrid` | Dense number matrix + horizontal/vertical operator arrays |
| `EquationId` | `record(Axis, lineIndex, equationIndex)` ‚Äî identifies a single equation |
| `EquationMask` | `Set<EquationId>` of hidden equations |
| `CrossMathGenerator` | Row-by-row generation: free rows + derived rows via backtracking |
| `PuzzlePrinter` | Dense grid console renderer |
| `CrossMathMain` | CLI entry point |

---

## Puzzle structure

### Grid layout

The display grid is `(2 √ó matrixSize ‚àí 1) √ó (2 √ó matrixSize ‚àí 1)`. Number cells
occupy even-row, even-column positions. Symbol cells (operators, `=` signs) fill
the positions between them. Odd-odd positions are empty spacers.

| matrixSize | Display | Approx equations |
|---|---|---|
| 3 | 5√ó5 | ~4 (educational) |
| 5 | 9√ó9 | ~10 (standard) |
| 7 | 13√ó13 | ~20 (hard) |

### Equation structure

Every equation is a chain read left-to-right (horizontal) or top-to-bottom (vertical):

```
operand‚ÇÄ  op‚ÇÄ  operand‚ÇÅ  =  result
```

**Result chaining:**

The dense matrix generator naturally produces `A op B = C op D = E` patterns
where the result of one equation feeds as an operand into the next. This is a
valid internal solution but is **not playable** as-is.

The `resultChainingPlayable` config field (default `false`) controls whether
these chains are shown to the player. When `false`, the masking layer must
ensure chained result equations are not both visible simultaneously ‚Äî though
they remain valid as internal solution combinations that can generate
additional puzzle variations.

### Intersection cells

In the dense matrix layout, every number cell at the junction of a horizontal
and vertical equation is an intersection. Its value must satisfy both equations.

---

## CrossMathGenerator

Dense matrix row-by-row generation using seeding + backtracking.

### Phase 1 ‚Äî Seed compatible column pairs

Free columns (col 0, 1, 3, 5, ‚Ä¶) are assigned to operators round-robin in
ascending usage order. For each column the assigned operator provides a valid
bottom value via `validRightOperands()`, guaranteeing that operator has at
least one usable (top, bottom) pair in the derived row.

### Phase 2 ‚Äî Fill free rows

Free rows (0, 1, 3, ‚Ä¶) choose their own values. The generator picks values
using a `BracketedPicker` that cycles through equal segments of the valid
range, ensuring variety. Operators are tried in ascending usage order so
underused operators get first pick.

Each row contains `equationsPerLine` chained equations:
```
[a]  op‚ÇÅ  [b]  =  [c]  op‚ÇÇ  [d]  =  [e]
```

### Phase 3 ‚Äî Derive constrained rows (backtracking)

Derived rows (2, 4, ‚Ä¶) are computed from two source rows above them. For each
column, the generator tries all registered operators to compute
`apply(topValue, bottomValue)`. Early pruning at result columns immediately
checks whether a valid horizontal equation exists, preventing dead-end
exploration.

### Phase 4 ‚Äî Seed next free row

After deriving a row, the generator seeds compatible bottom values for the
next free row using round-robin operator assignment, ensuring operator
diversity propagates through all derived rows.

### Post-generation checks

1. **Operator usage minimum** ‚Äî every registered operator must appear at least
   `config.minUsagePerOperator` times across all equations.
2. **Verification** ‚Äî `PuzzleGrid.verify()` independently recomputes every
   horizontal and vertical equation.

---

## Operator interface

```java
public interface Operator {
    char symbol();
    int apply(int leftOperand, int rightOperand, PuzzleConfig config);
    List<Integer> validRightOperands(int leftOperand, PuzzleConfig config, Random random);
    String constraints();
    default boolean isUnary() { return false; }
}
```

### `apply()` contract
Returns the valid result or `Integer.MIN_VALUE` on any constraint violation.
All constraint logic lives here ‚Äî the generator has zero per-operator conditionals.

### `validRightOperands()` contract
Returns **all** valid right operands for the given left operand, shuffled.
Must enumerate the exact valid domain (not sample). The generator iterates
the full list before abandoning the operator ‚Äî this prevents false rejection
when one value fails but another would work.

### Standard operators

| Op | Key constraints |
|---|---|
| `+` | Both operands ‚â• minCellValue; sum ‚àà [min, max] |
| `‚àí` | difference ‚â• minCellValue (no negatives or zero; left > right implied) |
| `√ó` | Both ‚â• 2; product ‚â§ maxCellValue; product NOT prime (BitSet sieve) |
| `√∑` | divisor ‚àà [2, maxCellValue/2]; exact integer; quotient ‚â• minCellValue |

### Adding custom operators

```java
registry.add(new MinOperator());   // already stubbed in CustomOperators.java
registry.add(new ExpOperator());
registry.remove('/');              // disable any operator
```

No other class changes. The `Operator` interface is the only extension point.

---

## PuzzleConfig ‚Äî derived constants

Core constants are computed from three root inputs: `matrixSize`, `minCellValue`,
`maxCellValue`. Per-operator caps are derived by default but can be overridden
in the builder for grading.

| Field | Default formula | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `equationsPerLine` | `(matrixSize-1)/2` | Chains per row/col |
| `maxAddOperand` | `maxCellValue/2` | Sum stays in bounds (overridable) |
| `maxMultiplyOperand` | `‚åä‚àömaxCellValue‚åã` | Product stays in bounds (overridable) |
| `maxDivisionDivisor` | `maxCellValue/2` | Prevents trivial √∑ (overridable) |
| `maxChainSafeOperand` | `maxCellValue/(2√ó(eqPerLine+1))` | Picker ceiling |
| `allowedOperators` | `null` (all) | Which operators to register |
| `resultChainingPlayable` | `false` | Show `a op b = c op d = e` to player? |

### Grading / masking fields

| Field | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `minVisibilityPercent` | 0.0 | Min visible cells for difficulty band |
| `maxVisibilityPercent` | 1.0 | Max visible cells for difficulty band |
| `operatorsCanBeHidden` | `true` | Early levels keep operators visible |
| `resultsCanBeHidden` | `true` | Early levels keep results visible |
| `maxUnknownsPerEquation` | unlimited | 1 for early levels |
| `numChoices` | 4 | Answer options (3‚Äì5) |

### Builder

```java
// Standard adult puzzle
PuzzleConfig config = PuzzleConfig.builder()
    .matrixSize(5)               // 5 ‚Üí 9√ó9 display; 3 ‚Üí 5√ó5; 7 ‚Üí 13√ó13
    .minCellValue(1)
    .maxCellValue(100)
    .maxGenerationAttempts(1000)
    .minUsagePerOperator(2)      // 0 = disabled; 1 = at least once; 2 = default
    .numBrackets(4)              // BracketedPicker segments
    .build();

// Level 0 ‚Äî first grade, addition only, numbers 0‚Äì10
PuzzleConfig level0 = PuzzleConfig.builder()
    .matrixSize(3)
    .minCellValue(0)
    .maxCellValue(10)
    .allowedOperators(Set.of('+'))
    .minUsagePerOperator(0)
    .operatorsCanBeHidden(false)
    .resultsCanBeHidden(false)
    .maxUnknownsPerEquation(1)
    .numChoices(3)
    .minVisibilityPercent(0.85)
    .maxVisibilityPercent(0.95)
    .build();

// Level 3 ‚Äî intro multiplication, numbers 0‚Äì50, times-2 table only
PuzzleConfig level3 = PuzzleConfig.builder()
    .matrixSize(5)
    .minCellValue(0)
    .maxCellValue(50)
    .allowedOperators(Set.of('+', '-', '*'))
    .maxMultiplyOperand(2)
    .numChoices(4)
    .minVisibilityPercent(0.55)
    .maxVisibilityPercent(0.65)
    .build();
```

---

## BracketedPicker

Divides `[minCellValue, maxChainSafeOperand]` into `numBrackets` equal segments
and cycles round-robin. Ensures large and small values both appear.

**Auto-fallback:** if `rangeSize < 2 √ó numBrackets`, reduces bracket count to
`max(1, rangeSize/2)` silently. Never throws for narrow ranges.

```
matrixSize=5, maxCellValue=100 ‚Üí range [1,10], 4 brackets ‚Üí [1‚Äì3][4‚Äì5][6‚Äì8][9‚Äì10]
matrixSize=5, maxCellValue=500 ‚Üí range [1,25], 4 brackets ‚Üí [1‚Äì7][8‚Äì13][14‚Äì19][20‚Äì25]
```

---

## EquationMask

Hides equations by `EquationId(Axis, lineIndex, equationIndex)`. A hidden
equation renders its operator and `=` symbols as blank, while number cells
remain visible (they still participate in perpendicular equations).

```java
EquationMask mask = EquationMask.allVisible();                    // solution display
EquationMask mask = EquationMask.random(config, 4, random);      // puzzle display
mask.hide(new EquationId(Axis.HORIZONTAL, 2, 1));                // hide specific equation
```

When `resultChainingPlayable` is false, the masking layer should ensure
that chained result equations are not simultaneously visible.

---

## CLI

```bash
# Compile
mkdir -p out
javac -d out src/io/crossmath/*.java

# Run
java -cp out io.crossmath.CrossMathMain [seed] [equationsToHide] [maxCellValue] [matrixSize] [minUsagePerOperator] [numBrackets]

# Examples
java -cp out io.crossmath.CrossMathMain                          # defaults
java -cp out io.crossmath.CrossMathMain 42                       # fixed seed
java -cp out io.crossmath.CrossMathMain 42 4 100 5 2 4          # full params
java -cp out io.crossmath.CrossMathMain 42 2 20 3 1 2           # easy 5√ó5, small numbers
java -cp out io.crossmath.CrossMathMain 42 6 500 7 2 4          # hard 13√ó13, large numbers
```

---

## Display example

A typical 9√ó9 output (dense matrix, matrixSize=5):

```
  [3]  +  [5]  =  [8]  -  [2]  =  [6]
   +       -       +       +       -
  [4]  +  [1]  =  [5]  +  [7]  = [12]
   =       =       =       =       =
  [7]  +  [4]  = [11]  -  [2]  =  [9]    ‚Üê derived from rows 0 & 1
   -       +       -       +       +
  [1]  +  [6]  =  [7]  -  [3]  =  [4]
   =       =       =       =       =
  [6]  + [10]  =  [4]  + [11]  =  [5]    ‚Üê derived from rows 2 & 3
```

Every row and column contains `equationsPerLine` valid equations.
Result chaining (`= [8] - [2] = [6]`) is valid internally but the
masking layer controls what is shown to the player.

---

## Efficiency

Generate valid equation triples under tight bounds, place them incrementally into a sparse intersecting shape, and use aggressive pruning plus operator-specific strategies to avoid brute force.

The algorithm time and space complex must be limited. For this, efficient data structures also algorithmic methods like pruning and backtracking must be utilized.

 
Example pruning rules for operators:

For a max value M:

Addition

Only generate pairs where:

a + b <= M

If a is fixed, then:

b <= M - a
Subtraction

If negatives are not allowed:

a >= b
a - b >= minValue

If b is fixed:

a >= b
Multiplication

Only generate pairs where:

a * b <= M

If a > 0, then:

b <= floor(M / a)

If zero is disallowed, exclude it early.

Division

Only exact division unless configured otherwise:

b != 0
a % b == 0
a / b <= M

Instead of choosing random a and b, generate division as:

choose divisor b
choose quotient c
derive dividend a = b * c

Add more constraints if needed.

## Gamification rules


To avoid boring puzzles, you may later bias away from:

too many repeated results
too many zeroes
too many ones
too many trivial equations like n + 0 = n or n * 1 = n
early resolution - using smaller numbers and resolving with only addition and substraction operators.
We added BracketedPicker to pick the numbers equally from range pools.

This is not just aesthetics; it also improves puzzle quality.

## Known constraints and trade-offs

### Multiply-only on large grids

Pure multiplication compounds exponentially. For multiply-only puzzles:

| matrixSize | Min maxCellValue |
|---|---|
| 3 | 16 |
| 5 | 512 |
| 7 | 65536 |

**Recommendation:** Do not use multiplication as the only operator. Pair it with
addition or subtraction.

### Small maxCellValue

For educational puzzles with small numbers, use `matrixSize(3)` with
`maxCellValue(20)` for a clean 5√ó5 display. Using `maxCellValue=10` with `matrixSize=5`
produces too many duplicate equations due to the narrow value space.

### minUsagePerOperator

- Default 2: reliable for all combinations of the 4 standard operators.
- Set to 0 when using a single operator (add-only, subtract-only, etc.).

---

## Future extensions planned

- **Grading layer:** use `PuzzleConfig` grading fields to generate age-appropriate
  puzzles per grade level (see `grades.txt`).
- **Asymmetric shapes / sparse puzzles:** the dense matrix is the internal solution;
  the masking layer can hide entire equations to create sparse, asymmetric playable
  shapes. Branching from any operand in any direction is valid as long as
  result chaining rules are respected.
- **Longer chains:** `A op B op C = result` chains within a single equation.
- **3-digit numbers:** use `maxCellValue(999)`.
- **New operators:** implement `Operator`, register via `registry.add()`.
  `Exp`, `Sqrt`, `Min`, `Max` stubs are in `CustomOperators.java`.
- **Puzzle difficulty scoring:** count hidden cells vs total, consider operator
  complexity.
- **JSON export:** serialize `PuzzleGrid` for use by frontend clients.
